texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Solubility of Nitrous Oxide in Amine Solutions
texts.text: Solubility of Nitrous Oxide in Amine Solutions

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Z. Bensetiti, I. Iliuta, F. Larachi,* and B. P. A. Grandjean
texts.text: Z. Bensetiti, I. Iliuta, F. Larachi,* and B. P. A. Grandjean

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Department of Chemical Engineering & CERPIC, Laval University, Que ´ bec, Canada G1K 7P4
texts.text: Department of Chemical Engineering & CERPIC, Laval University, Que ´ bec, Canada G1K 7P4

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The solubility of nitrous oxide (N2O) in 13 amine solvents and solutions was correlated to amine mole fractions and temperature using feedforward neural networks. This general correlation, using a massive database, predicted N2O solubility at temperatures between 283 and 398 K in pure solvents [H2O, monoethanolamine (MEA), diethanolamine (DEA), methyldiethanolamine (MDEA), and 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanolamine (AMP)], in binary aqueous amine solutions [H2O/MEA, H2O/DEA, H2O/MDEA, and H2O/AMP], and in ternary aqueous amine blends [AMP/ MDEA/H2O, AMP/DEA/H2O, DEA/MDEA/H2O, MDEA/MEA/H2O, and AMP/MEA/H2O]. Combined with the 'N2O analogy', this present improved correlation can be advantageously implemented in amine plant design software and procedures for the prediction of CO2 solubility in amine blend solutions over wide temperature and concentration ranges.
texts.text: The solubility of nitrous oxide (N2O) in 13 amine solvents and solutions was correlated to amine mole fractions and temperature using feedforward neural networks. This general correlation, using a massive database, predicted N2O solubility at temperatures between 283 and 398 K in pure solvents [H2O, monoethanolamine (MEA), diethanolamine (DEA), methyldiethanolamine (MDEA), and 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanolamine (AMP)], in binary aqueous amine solutions [H2O/MEA, H2O/DEA, H2O/MDEA, and H2O/AMP], and in ternary aqueous amine blends [AMP/ MDEA/H2O, AMP/DEA/H2O, DEA/MDEA/H2O, MDEA/MEA/H2O, and AMP/MEA/H2O]. Combined with the 'N2O analogy', this present improved correlation can be advantageously implemented in amine plant design software and procedures for the prediction of CO2 solubility in amine blend solutions over wide temperature and concentration ranges.

texts.text: molecular volume, and electronic structure, N2O is often used as a nonreacting gas to mimic CO2. This is done through the so-called 'N2O analogy'. 6

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Many investigators measured the solubility of N2O in water, AMP, DEA, MDEA, MEA, and the mixtures thereof. 7 -26 The following single- and multiple-component systems were explored: H2O, 7 -9,11,13,15,18,21 -25 AMP, 20 DEA, 20 MEA, 20 MDEA, 20 H2O/DEA, 9,10,12,13,16,17,19,22 H2O/ MEA, 8,9,12,19,20,22,24,25 H2O/MDEA, 11 -15,22 -25 H2O/ AMP, 18,19,21,23,25 AMP/MEA/H2O, 25 AMP/DEA/H2O, 26 AMP/MDEA/H2O, 20,23 DEA/MDEA/H2O, 19,26 and MDEA/ MEA/H2O. 24,25 Despite the abundant literature data on N2O solubility in water and in amine solutions, it is noteworthy to recognize that there is no general correlation for N2O solubility in amine solutions. Except for attempts by few investigators, 13,15,16,19,20,28 most of the literature on N2O solubility in amine solutions is of an experimental nature. The Twente group 13,16,19 correlated N2O solubility data using polynomial functions. Al-Ghawas et al. 15 proposed a 12-parameter polynomial relationship for the solubility of N2O in a H2O/MDEA system. Wang et al. 20 developed an excess Henry constant semiempirical model for a N2O Henry constant in various binary and ternary aqueous amines and amine mixtures over wide ranges of temperatures and concentrations. Li and Mather 28 extended the scaledparticle theory 29 to correlate the solubility of N2O in binary and ternary amine systems based on the N2O -pure solvent solubility data (energy and size parameters).
texts.text: Many investigators measured the solubility of N2O in water, AMP, DEA, MDEA, MEA, and the mixtures thereof. 7 -26 The following single- and multiple-component systems were explored: H2O, 7 -9,11,13,15,18,21 -25 AMP, 20 DEA, 20 MEA, 20 MDEA, 20 H2O/DEA, 9,10,12,13,16,17,19,22 H2O/ MEA, 8,9,12,19,20,22,24,25 H2O/MDEA, 11 -15,22 -25 H2O/ AMP, 18,19,21,23,25 AMP/MEA/H2O, 25 AMP/DEA/H2O, 26 AMP/MDEA/H2O, 20,23 DEA/MDEA/H2O, 19,26 and MDEA/ MEA/H2O. 24,25 Despite the abundant literature data on N2O solubility in water and in amine solutions, it is noteworthy to recognize that there is no general correlation for N2O solubility in amine solutions. Except for attempts by few investigators, 13,15,16,19,20,28 most of the literature on N2O solubility in amine solutions is of an experimental nature. The Twente group 13,16,19 correlated N2O solubility data using polynomial functions. Al-Ghawas et al. 15 proposed a 12-parameter polynomial relationship for the solubility of N2O in a H2O/MDEA system. Wang et al. 20 developed an excess Henry constant semiempirical model for a N2O Henry constant in various binary and ternary aqueous amines and amine mixtures over wide ranges of temperatures and concentrations. Li and Mather 28 extended the scaledparticle theory 29 to correlate the solubility of N2O in binary and ternary amine systems based on the N2O -pure solvent solubility data (energy and size parameters).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Relying on artificial neural networks and an exhaustive N2O solubility database, the present work endeavors to develop an improved and updated correlation of N2O solubility in water, AMP, DEA, MDEA, MEA, and their mixtures over wide ranges of amine concentrations and temperatures. The capability of the neural correlation will be illustrated by (i) comparing its predictions to the recent solubility thermodynamic models and (ii) its capacity to restore, via the 'N2O analogy', actual CO2 solubility measurements reported in the literature for short contact time devices.
texts.text: Relying on artificial neural networks and an exhaustive N2O solubility database, the present work endeavors to develop an improved and updated correlation of N2O solubility in water, AMP, DEA, MDEA, MEA, and their mixtures over wide ranges of amine concentrations and temperatures. The capability of the neural correlation will be illustrated by (i) comparing its predictions to the recent solubility thermodynamic models and (ii) its capacity to restore, via the 'N2O analogy', actual CO2 solubility measurements reported in the literature for short contact time devices.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A broad spectrum of alkanolamines and, more recently, their mixtures are being employed for the removal of acid gases such as CO2, H2S, and COS, from natural gas, syngas streams, oil refineries, synthetic ammonia industries, and so forth. Because of their high reactivity and low cost, ease of reclamation, and low absorption of hydrocarbons, monoethanolamine (MEA) and diethanolamine (DEA) are among the most widely used amines. However, highly concentrated MEA solutions are to be avoided in CO2 abatement as these solutions are known to be aggressive and may react with unrelated materials such as reactor vessels, tubing lines, and several process compartments. DEA, in turn, is known to form carbamates when reacting with CO2 which reduces its absorption capacity. Methyldiethanolamine (MDEA), a tertiary amine, has proven appropriate for the selective removal of H2S from gas streams containing CO2 and H2S. 1,2 Also, compared to common primary and secondary amines, MDEA offers higher absorption capacities. 3 Another potential absorbent is the 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP) amine, a sterically hindered primary amine, which has been shown to inhibit the formation of carbamates. 4 AMP has the same CO2 absorption capacity as MDEA but has a higher reaction rate constant for the reaction with CO2. Blends of primary and tertiary amines have been shown to outperform the conventional single-amine solutions. 5 Blended amines combine the absorption characteristics of the constituent amines such as higher loading capacities and faster reaction rates. The merits of amine blends may result in treated gases with higher purity, lower energy consumption, lower economic cost, and a tunable absorption selectivity. To become potential substitutes in sour gas treating processes, the properties of amine blends, such as those involving MEA, DEA, MDEA, and AMP, need to be accurately quantified via theoretical models or correlations over wide ranges of amine concentrations and temperature. For instance, the design of gas -liquid absorbers for carbon dioxide removal in amine blends requires the precise knowledge of CO2 (physical) solubility. For most mixed solvents and amine blends, owing to the chemical reactions that occur in the system, it is difficult to obtain this solubility in a direct way. In view of its similar configuration,
texts.text: A broad spectrum of alkanolamines and, more recently, their mixtures are being employed for the removal of acid gases such as CO2, H2S, and COS, from natural gas, syngas streams, oil refineries, synthetic ammonia industries, and so forth. Because of their high reactivity and low cost, ease of reclamation, and low absorption of hydrocarbons, monoethanolamine (MEA) and diethanolamine (DEA) are among the most widely used amines. However, highly concentrated MEA solutions are to be avoided in CO2 abatement as these solutions are known to be aggressive and may react with unrelated materials such as reactor vessels, tubing lines, and several process compartments. DEA, in turn, is known to form carbamates when reacting with CO2 which reduces its absorption capacity. Methyldiethanolamine (MDEA), a tertiary amine, has proven appropriate for the selective removal of H2S from gas streams containing CO2 and H2S. 1,2 Also, compared to common primary and secondary amines, MDEA offers higher absorption capacities. 3 Another potential absorbent is the 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP) amine, a sterically hindered primary amine, which has been shown to inhibit the formation of carbamates. 4 AMP has the same CO2 absorption capacity as MDEA but has a higher reaction rate constant for the reaction with CO2. Blends of primary and tertiary amines have been shown to outperform the conventional single-amine solutions. 5 Blended amines combine the absorption characteristics of the constituent amines such as higher loading capacities and faster reaction rates. The merits of amine blends may result in treated gases with higher purity, lower energy consumption, lower economic cost, and a tunable absorption selectivity. To become potential substitutes in sour gas treating processes, the properties of amine blends, such as those involving MEA, DEA, MDEA, and AMP, need to be accurately quantified via theoretical models or correlations over wide ranges of amine concentrations and temperature. For instance, the design of gas -liquid absorbers for carbon dioxide removal in amine blends requires the precise knowledge of CO2 (physical) solubility. For most mixed solvents and amine blends, owing to the chemical reactions that occur in the system, it is difficult to obtain this solubility in a direct way. In view of its similar configuration,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Neural Regression. A three-layer feedforward neural network was retained as a regression model for the nitrous oxide solubility. Its architecture is illustrated schematically in Figure 1. The input variables of the neural network consisted of the mole fractions of AMP, DEA, MDEA, and MEA, respectively, U 1, U 2, U 3, and U 4. Each amine corresponded then to a specific input node and was described by its actual mole fraction in the solution. The fifth input node, U 5, was used to represent the temperature at which the solubility is to be estimated. Table 1 lists the set of equations of the neural correlation wherein the network output (normalized N2O Henry constant) was correlated to the 5 input variables. The NNfit software 27 was used to develop the neural correlation.
texts.text: Neural Regression. A three-layer feedforward neural network was retained as a regression model for the nitrous oxide solubility. Its architecture is illustrated schematically in Figure 1. The input variables of the neural network consisted of the mole fractions of AMP, DEA, MDEA, and MEA, respectively, U 1, U 2, U 3, and U 4. Each amine corresponded then to a specific input node and was described by its actual mole fraction in the solution. The fifth input node, U 5, was used to represent the temperature at which the solubility is to be estimated. Table 1 lists the set of equations of the neural correlation wherein the network output (normalized N2O Henry constant) was correlated to the 5 input variables. The NNfit software 27 was used to develop the neural correlation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: least-squares regression on a set of input and output data taken from the solubility database. To overcome the over-fitting problem which may alter the network generalization capabilities, the database was split into two sets of historical data. The first set of historical data, obtained from randomly sampling 70% of the experimental data, served for the identification of the weighting factors and for the training of the neural correlation. The second set of historical data was then used to test the neural correlation on how well it was trained. To determine the optimal number of hidden nodes, J was varied from 2 to 10. The optimal network was selected as being the best compromise between too little training resulting in an inaccurate correlation and too much training leading to a correlation too sensitive to eventual inconsistent data scattered in the database.
texts.text: least-squares regression on a set of input and output data taken from the solubility database. To overcome the over-fitting problem which may alter the network generalization capabilities, the database was split into two sets of historical data. The first set of historical data, obtained from randomly sampling 70% of the experimental data, served for the identification of the weighting factors and for the training of the neural correlation. The second set of historical data was then used to test the neural correlation on how well it was trained. To determine the optimal number of hidden nodes, J was varied from 2 to 10. The optimal network was selected as being the best compromise between too little training resulting in an inaccurate correlation and too much training leading to a correlation too sensitive to eventual inconsistent data scattered in the database.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this model, He 1,m is the Henry constant of N2O absorbed in the mixed solvent, He 1, i is the Henry constant of N2O absorbed in the pure solvent i , … i is the volume fraction of solvent i , and R ij and R ijk are, respectively, the two-body and three-body interaction parameters. To use this model, knowledge of the pure solvent Henry constants is required. Also, to perform Henry constant computations for the systems belonging to the solubility database, 23 fitted parameters are required by this model 20,25,26 (see Table 2 for the list of model parameters).

texts.orig: The two aforementioned solubility thermodynamic models were also critically evaluated using the same database of tabulated Henry constants. An analysis of Table 6 shows that generally the present neural net correlation outperformed the two tested solubility models. In particular, a net improvement was attained in the case of the mixed amine solutions (i.e., binary and
texts.text: The two aforementioned solubility thermodynamic models were also critically evaluated using the same database of tabulated Henry constants. An analysis of Table 6 shows that generally the present neural net correlation outperformed the two tested solubility models. In particular, a net improvement was attained in the case of the mixed amine solutions (i.e., binary and

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: ternary solutions). Not surprisingly, however, the application of the Wang et al. 20 model on Li and Lee's 26 own solubility measurements (10th and 12th entries of Table 6) gave better predictions since only those data served in the identification of the two-body and the three-body interaction parameters by Li and Lee 26 themselves. In the case of the pure solvents, N2O solubilities were well-correlated using the Li and Mather 28 model and the neural network correlation alike. It is worth pinpointing that while the same number of fitted parameters is required in the three approaches, the nitrous oxide solubility was generally better estimated from the neural net correlation. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, the improved N2O solubility correlation proposed in this work could be advantageously implemented in amine plant design software and procedures for the prediction of N2O solubility in amine solutions within the ranges of amine composition and temperature shown in Table 4. The neural N2O solubility correlation can be combined, for instance, with the correlation of Versteeg et al., 13 to estimate CO2 solubility in the above amine solutions via the N2O analogy. 6 As an illustration, Figure 3 is a parity plot of predicted (via N2O analogy) versus measured CO2 Henry constants for water and MDEA aqueous solutions. For CO2 absorption in water, MARE was 5.40% (5.20% for Versteeg et al. 13 data; 5.90% for Al-Ghawas et al. 14 data; 6.40% for Li and Lai 25 data), and ARE was 2.40%. For CO2 absorption in MDEA, MARE was 4.70% and ARE was 3.40%.
texts.text: Finally, the improved N2O solubility correlation proposed in this work could be advantageously implemented in amine plant design software and procedures for the prediction of N2O solubility in amine solutions within the ranges of amine composition and temperature shown in Table 4. The neural N2O solubility correlation can be combined, for instance, with the correlation of Versteeg et al., 13 to estimate CO2 solubility in the above amine solutions via the N2O analogy. 6 As an illustration, Figure 3 is a parity plot of predicted (via N2O analogy) versus measured CO2 Henry constants for water and MDEA aqueous solutions. For CO2 absorption in water, MARE was 5.40% (5.20% for Versteeg et al. 13 data; 5.90% for Al-Ghawas et al. 14 data; 6.40% for Li and Lai 25 data), and ARE was 2.40%. For CO2 absorption in MDEA, MARE was 4.70% and ARE was 3.40%.
